1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlî napšî ʾet-yhwh — "Praise the LORD! Praise the LORD, O my soul!" — Ps 146 opens the Hallelujah Quintet (Ps 146-150), each psalm beginning and ending with hallĕlûyāh. The self-address hallĕlî napšî (praise the LORD, O my soul) — the soul (nepeš) called to praise matches Psalms 103:1-2 (same self-exhortation). The psalmist commands his own interior life to align with worship — the will engaging the emotions and the whole living self in the act of praise.
2: ʾăhallĕlāh yhwh bĕḥayyāy ʾăzammĕrāh lĕʾĕlōhay bĕʿôdî — "I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praises to my God as long as I live" — bĕḥayyāy (while I live, in my life) + bĕʿôdî (as long as I exist, while I am yet) — the two temporal phrases expressing lifelong praise. This stands in deliberate contrast to vv3-4: human princes have no life beyond death (their plans perish), but the psalmist's praise extends to the full length of his life. The reliability of YHWH (v6-9) is the ground for a life of praise.
3: ʾal-tibtĕḥû binnĕdîbîm bĕben-ʾādām šeʾên lô tĕšûʿāh — "do not put your trust in princes, in any human being, for there is no salvation in them" — nĕdîbîm (princes, nobles, willing/generous ones) — the highest rank of human power. ben-ʾādām (son of Adam, a human being) — the mortality-marker; humans are sons of Adam, subject to Adam's curse. šeʾên lô tĕšûʿāh (in whom there is no salvation/rescue) — tĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance, from yāšaʿ) — the same root as yĕhôšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus). No human being, however powerful, has the capacity to save; tĕšûʿāh belongs only to YHWH (v7-9).
4: tēṣēʾ rûḥô yāšub lĕʾadmātô bayyôm hahûʾ ʾābĕdû ʿešĕtōnōtāyw — "when his breath/spirit departs, he returns to his earth/soil; on that very day his plans come to nothing" — tēṣēʾ rûḥô (his spirit/breath departs) — rûaḥ as the animating breath; when it departs, the body becomes a corpse. yāšub lĕʾadmātô (he returns to his earth/soil) — the reverse of Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathed into the ʾādām made from ʾadāmāh); death is that creation reversed. bayyôm hahûʾ ʾābĕdû ʿešĕtōnōtāyw (on that very day his plans/thoughts perish) — ʿĕšĕtōnôt (plans, schemes, thoughts, from ʿāšat) — the most powerful person's best-laid plans evaporate the moment the breath leaves. This makes trust in princes not merely imprudent but structurally futile.
5: ʾašrê šeʾēl yaʿăqōb bĕʿezrô śibrô ʿal-yhwh ʾĕlōhāyw — "blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD his God" — ʾašrê (blessed, happy) — the beatitude form. In deliberate contrast to v3 (trust in princes = futile), v5 offers the alternative: help from the God of Jacob. ʾēl yaʿăqōb (the God of Jacob) — the patriarchal title emphasizing covenant continuity; Jacob's God is the God of the powerless-made-powerful, the wrestler, the schemer redeemed. śibrô ʿal-yhwh (whose hope is placed on the LORD) — śābar (to hope, wait, expect — same as Psalms 145:15) — the posture of expectant trust.
6: ʿōśeh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ ʾet-hayyām wĕʾet-kol-ʾăšer-bām haššōmēr ʾĕmet lĕʿôlām — "who made heaven and earth, the sea and everything in them, and who keeps faith forever" — the classic creator-title ʿōśeh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) — appearing also in Psalms 115:15; Psalms 121:2; Psalms 124:8 — grounds the help of v5 in creation sovereignty. The creator who made all things can rescue within what he made. haššōmēr ʾĕmet lĕʿôlām (the one who keeps faith forever) — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness, reliability) + šāmar (to keep, guard) — YHWH guards his own faithfulness; it is not something that can be violated or erode. Unlike the prince whose plans perish in a day (v4), YHWH's faithfulness is permanent.
7: ʿōśeh mišpāṭ laʿăšûqîm nôtēn leḥem lārĕʿēbîm yhwh mattîr ʾăsûrîm — "who secures justice for the oppressed and gives food to the hungry; the LORD sets prisoners free" — three divine actions in one verse: ʿōśeh mišpāṭ laʿăšûqîm (doing justice for the oppressed) — ʿāšaq (to oppress, squeeze, defraud) — those who have been wronged in the legal and economic system. nôtēn leḥem lārĕʿēbîm (giving bread to the hungry) — the provision of basic sustenance as a divine act. yhwh mattîr ʾăsûrîm (the LORD sets free those who are bound) — āsar (to bind, imprison). The six divine actions for the vulnerable in vv7-9 form the basis for Jesus' Nazareth manifesto (Luke 4:18-19) and the beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) — liberation of precisely these categories of people signals the arrival of the kingdom.
8: yhwh pōqēaḥ ʿiwwĕrîm yhwh zōqēp kĕpûpîm yhwh ʾōhēb ṣaddîqîm — "the LORD gives sight to the blind; the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down; the LORD loves the righteous" — pōqēaḥ ʿiwwĕrîm (opening [the eyes of] the blind, from pāqaḥ, to open eyes, ears) — the opening of eyes as divine work (Isaiah 35:5; Psalms 42:7). Jesus' healing of the blind (Matthew 11:5; John 9) is presented as fulfillment of this prophetic-psalmic pattern. yhwh zōqēp kĕpûpîm (the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down) — same as Psalms 145:14 (zôqēp kĕpûpîm), making the connection explicit. yhwh ʾōhēb ṣaddîqîm (the LORD loves the righteous) — the love of YHWH is not merely obligatory covenant action but genuine ʾahăbāh (love).
9: yhwh šōmēr ʾet-gērîm yātôm wĕʾalmānāh yĕʿôdēd wĕderek rĕšāʿîm yĕʿawwēt — "the LORD watches over the alien resident; he supports the fatherless and the widow; but the way of the wicked he overturns/makes crooked" — the three classic vulnerable categories: gēr (alien/stranger resident, one without full civic rights), yātôm (orphan, the fatherless), and ʾalmānāh (widow) — the legal-social triad of those without a male protector, repeatedly named in Torah (Exodus 22:21-22; Deuteronomy 27:19). yĕʿôdēd (he supports, sustains, from ʿûd, to support, restore) — YHWH as the structural support replacing the missing human protector. wĕderek rĕšāʿîm yĕʿawwēt (the way of the wicked he overturns/makes crooked) — the ironic use of ʿāwah (to twist, bend, pervert — used of injustice): the God who straightens the crooked paths of the poor perverts the plans of the wicked.
10: yimlōk yhwh lĕʿôlām ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn lĕdōr wādōr hallĕlû-yāh — "the LORD will reign forever — your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD!" — yimlōk yhwh lĕʿôlām (the LORD will reign forever) — the eternal kingship declaration closes the psalm: unlike the prince of v3 whose plans die with his breath, YHWH's reign has no term. ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn (your God, O Zion) — the universal sovereign is specifically Zion's covenant God; the cosmic and the particular are held together. hallĕlû-yāh — closing the first of the Quintet and opening the path to Pss 147-150, the great doxological finale of the entire Psalter.
